result:
The Hour Exchange sits on the corner where the street forgets where it was going. It’s an old brick building with a green door that sticks in winter and a bell that rings like a teaspoon in a cup. Most people pass by it dozens of times before they notice the sign, hand-painted, hung crooked above the lintel: Time lent here. No collateral but care.

Inside it smells like rain in the wood. The floorboards know the weight of hurry. We keep our hours behind the counter in a series of narrow drawers, each sixty minutes long, each with its own faint hum. They are not clocks. They’re more like loaves, still warm from the oven of the second hand. I am the keeper, the one with the ledger. My name is not particularly important to the work.

People arrive holding their day with two hands and say, like a question, I think I need more.

Our job is not to judge, only to listen, to ask a few careful questions, to explain the price. You cannot pay us back with money. It takes a memory. Not any memory. Not the knowledge of your own name, not the faces of your dead, not the taste of water. We are stewards, not thieves. The exchange is equitable only when the thing surrendered nests gently in the palm. You choose. You decide what you can stand to go without.

When the baker came in on a Tuesday, flour dusting his eyelashes pale, he said, I need one hour for dough and one for the heat to find its courage. He paid us later with the memory of a song he used to hum at dawn in a kitchen where the windows had no curtains. It was his, unneeded now, he told me, because the rush had drowned the tune. I slipped the memory into a small paper envelope and wrote the title the way he said it, not trusting myself to spell it the way it deserved.

There are rules. We cannot lend you more than seven hours in a week. We cannot lend you time for harm, though what counts as harm is a terribly thin line some days. We are wary of lending time for apologies, too, because people think the extra sixty minutes will stitch humility onto them like a patch. Apologies are work, not length. I have learned that by watching faces go out and come back again.

Behind the building, in the walled garden that used to be vines and broken glass and is now something else entirely, we plant the memories in furrows. This is not for magic. The first time I did it, I believed I was soothing myself, giving the taken thing a good end. But sometimes, weeks later, there will be a thin sprout that smells like lemon peel and pencil shavings and the particular angle of a late September sun on a schoolyard. The garden is a rumor in the neighborhood, a place where time seeds might grow like mint. The bees move in it as if they’ve known it forever.

On a damp evening in early spring a woman came in with hair the color of rivers after a melt. She asked for a single hour. When I asked what she needed it for, she said the word wait carefully, as if it were a porcelain cup. She was waiting, she said, for a message from her brother in a country where messages go missing. She couldn’t bear to go to bed not knowing.

I slid her a soft-wrapped hour, the kind that won’t catch and tear on the rough edges of a night. She signed the ledger, both hands shaking, though she tried to make it elegant. In return she chose a memory that made her laugh at herself, she told me: a lime green scarf she wore for an entire winter when she was nineteen and for some reason believed she could be someone else just by wearing something loud. I wrote it down as scarf, lime, age nineteen, and a smudge of winter heat. I did not tell her what I wanted to tell her, which is that nothing you wear changes the soil under your feet but sometimes it changes the way the rain finds you.

She returned at dawn. The door stuck. Her palm on the green paint left a mark the shape of an island. I looked up and knew immediately something was wrong, not in her face, which was calmer now, emptied out from its clatter, but in the way she was looking past me as if the far wall had receded.

Did you get it? I asked, nodding toward the sky I could not see yet. Did the message come?

She said yes, and we were both so relieved we grinned like idiots, but then she said, very quietly, The stars moved.

She meant it. I could tell by the way she adjusted the sleeves of her coat, one, two, thumb through cuff, as if the motion would anchor her to the old pattern of things. We stepped outside and looked up together. Pale morning, a thin orange at the edge, the moon like a nicked coin. The constellations, if they had rearranged themselves, were above the bright, out of reach of the eyes we had on our faces at that hour. I told her it must be the leftover night in her pupils. She shook her head. No, she said. It’s not what I see. It’s what I know how to see. The handles are on the wrong sides of the pots and I can’t remember which season belongs to which set of stars.

We stood silent a minute and I let the bell ring closed behind me. This is a thing the work does to you. It makes you superstitious about quiet, about the seams where one kind of hour stitches into another.

By lunchtime I realized she was right in at least one way. A boy came in with dirt on his knees to ask if we had any extra for after-school because his mother’s bus was late and he didn’t want to go home to the hollow apartment. He wore the kind of bravery that is just a shirt someone grows into when they don’t have a choice. I gave him thirty minutes and a peppermint. When he signed, he wrote the loops on his letters backward and the top of his f became a tiny wave and I felt, as clearly as a shiver, that the alphabet itself had leaned a fraction to one side, the way pictures do in a hall where a door has slammed. Small, nearly nothing, but enough to call it a change.

You start to understand, working here, that there aren’t extra hours hidden behind the molding. Every time someone borrows one, the world shuffles. It takes the minute you didn’t miss yesterday, in the line at the post office where you were held by the way the sunlight turned everyone’s hands into sugar glass, and slides it quiet as a handkerchief into someone else’s pocket. And when you pay us back with memory, you’re not only putting something into the ground. You’re pruning yourself. Time grows in those cuts. There is a tenderness to it. There is a cost.

I did not start this work to change anything so large as the sky. I started it because my mother was beginning to forget the edges of things and I thought, stupidly, that if I handled time with care for strangers all day, the world would reciprocate at home. It did not. My mother forgot the name of our street in June and, in August, my birthday. She did not forget the song she sang to me when I was small, but that was because she forgot that singing was separate from speaking and sometimes she would turn to me and ask me, in a lullaby voice in the dairy aisle, where the eggs had gone. We sat together in that aisle a very long time.

I told myself I would never borrow an hour. The ledger has a column for staff, too, but I left it clean. It felt like a kind of fidelity, or maybe a punishment. Either way, I kept my hands folded on top of that promise.

Then a man came in, middle of the afternoon, rain trying very hard at the windows. He asked for two hours to sit by his father’s hospital bed without the mechanics of the day ripping him away. He said he could pay with a memory of the day his son was born and the nurse put the baby in the crook of his arm and he felt the part of him he had borrowed from his own father come back for a minute and yawn. Don’t, I said, so quickly it surprised both of us. Don’t give that. Keep it. Give us the shirt your brother stole from you when you were teenagers, the taste of the gum you chewed when you learned to drive, the precise weight of a hammer you bought and never learned to use. But keep that.

He laughed, then he cried, then he understood. He gave me the rust smell of a borrowed bike in May with streamers on the handlebars. I planted it the next morning, two green knuckles pushing up in a week, leaves shiny as if they had just remembered how to be touched.

On the first day of the first frost, I went to the ledger and signed my name. One hour. Purpose: to sit with my mother while she slept, to memorize the way her breath makes and unmakes a small winter on the window.

What memory? the field asked me.

I walked out to the garden for the answer. The vines were brittle; the bees had gone to where bees dream. I pressed my hands into the dirt and knew. I wrote it in the careful square of the column: the taste of linden honey from the summer I was nine, the year we lived in the green house and someone cut the tree back and the whole street smelled like tea. I chose it because it was dear enough to matter and because I could live without it if I was careful. I folded it into its envelope and held it awhile. It warmed in my hand and then cooled.

I thought it would leave a clean absence. That’s another thing the work teaches you: how to be wrong with grace. In the weeks after, I found I still remembered the jar on the counter, the way the light went through it into the wall, how at breakfast it made a gold flag on the bread. But I could not summon the taste, no matter how I closed my eyes and leaned toward it. The memory stayed as a picture without a mouth. I wasn’t sad, not exactly. I went to sit with my mother and that hour lay down between us like an extra blanket. It made room. It let me be small beside her without the doorbell of the future ringing.

In spring, unexpected, the city planted lindens along our block. Or the city did nothing and the ground, remembering, offered up saplings of its own accord. The bees came home. They crawled into the bell and made it sweet. Children tied ribbons to the branches. It has been that kind of year.

The woman with the river hair stops by from time to time. We talk about the sky like it’s a book the town is reading together. She says she has learned a new map for it, a map that accounts for the slight shift, which she is not sure was in the sky or in her. She carries a lime scarf in her bag to lend to anyone who needs something loud for a while.

We keep lending. We keep planting. The ledger fills and makes its quiet columns. Some days I think of it as a prayer. Some days as an inventory. The bell keeps ringing, small and stubborn, and the green door learns the names of everyone’s hands.

If you come, I’ll ask you what you need, and maybe it will be an hour and maybe it will be news, or for someone to tell you that the stars have always moved, we only notice when we pause. We can give you the pause. That part is easy. The rest, we make together, by choosing what to keep and what to put in the ground.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
